name: Jacob Boaz
ai_summary: Jacob Boaz brings 6 years of childcare experience and emphasizes safety-first conflict resolution by separating parties and understanding root causes. Based in Tucson, AZ with a 10-15 mile radius availability, he is passionate about providing male role models for Gen Z students and tailoring his approach to modern classroom needs.

transcripts.q3: So my name is Jacob. Oh, as I'm 23, I'm almost 24. I've been working in childcare for about six years. Ish, give or take. I've worked with kids being young is like one and a half to two. My work with high schoolers. The only group of kids. I would say I haven't worked with is like middle schoolers and like a true school-age setting. And then one of the biggest things I think for me is like I really think that it's important that we have a lot of generate like gen Z teachers because, you know, we're the ones who are growing up in the world right now. And I think it's best for our generation to be teaching the Next Generation because we're the ones dealing with what's going on right now. So for able to help out our future generation, I think the world will be a better better place.
transcripts.q4: So, I live in Tucson Arizona, and I mean, I can travel around Tucson. So wherever that may be mean preferably probably not farther than about 15, 10 to 15 miles is about far. But other than that, I'd be fine wherever.
transcripts.q5: I prefer full time. I'd like something that's Monday through Friday. Again, I'm fine with picking up shifts forever. So to me it doesn't really matter as long as I'm able to pick up shifts Monday through Friday. So with that being said probably full-time. Like I'd like to be able to pick up shifts Monday through Friday but if not then I could probably work around that.
transcripts.q6: So the biggest thing was that, I think would be making first off, is making sure the children are safe. That's like number one, is making sure. You know, do we need to separate what we need to do. The second thing is talking to both sides and trying to figure out, not necessarily like we already know what happened. We gotta figure out why it happened. So, really trying to understand why and not being focused on the negative, but more being focused on the understanding part of it. And then at that point, you know, if I need to write An incident report I will. Otherwise, you know you go on about the day if we need to let parents know, let director know. Let somebody else know who's higher up, that's fine. It's just kind of all depends on the situation.
transcripts.q7: I think I would make a great up kid teacher because I think that one of the biggest things is as a male. I think we don't have enough male positive role models as well as teachers and I think that having male teachers is really important, so I think I bring that to the table just by being a male. I also think that being a younger teacher like I have a lot of energy as well as I kind of, you know, I've been in school recently, I understand What kids are probably more into tune now than what they would have been, you know, maybe 30 years ago or something like that. So I think that providing that younger generation with them. Gives them a sense of oh this person's like like me like yeah they're an adult. But they're also still young. Like I kind of can resonate with this person a little bit and then finally, I think at the end of the day, like in my own personal mind, you know, my biggest interest is the kid. This is what can I provide for for the kid to be successful? What can we provide for them? So, I think that's like my biggest motivation to be a good teacher. And my biggest reason why I want to teach
